lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Romeo, come forth; come forth, thou fearful man:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Affliction is enamour’d of thy parts,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And thou art wedded to calamity.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Father, what news? what is the prince’s doom?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: What sorrow craves acquaintance at my hand,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: That I yet know not?

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Too familiar

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Is my dear son with such sour company:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: I bring thee tidings of the prince’s doom.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: What less than dooms-day is the prince’s doom?

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: A gentler judgment vanish’d from his lips,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Not body’s death, but body’s banishment.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Ha, banishment! be merciful, say “death;”

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: For exile hath more terror in his look,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Much more than death: do not say “banishment.”

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Hence from Verona art thou banished:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Be patient, for the world is broad and wide.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: There is no world without Verona walls,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: But purgatory, torture, hell itself.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Hence-banished is banish’d from the world,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And world’s exile is death: then banished,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Is death mis-term’d: calling death banishment,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Thou cutt’st my head off with a golden axe,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And smilest upon the stroke that murders me.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: O deadly sin! O rude unthankfulness!

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Thy fault our law calls death; but the kind prince,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Taking thy part, hath rush’d aside the law,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And turn’d that black word death to banishment:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: This is dear mercy, and thou seest it not.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: ’Tis torture, and not mercy: heaven is here,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Where Juliet lives; and every cat and dog

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And little mouse, every unworthy thing,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Live here in heaven and may look on her;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: But Romeo may not: more validity,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: More honourable state, more courtship lives

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: In carrion-flies than Romeo: they may seize

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: On the white wonder of dear Juliet’s hand

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And steal immortal blessing from her lips,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Who even in pure and vestal modesty,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Still blush, as thinking their own kisses sin;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: But Romeo may not; he is banished:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Flies may do this, but I from this must fly:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: They are free men, but I am banished.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And say’st thou yet that exile is not death?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Hadst thou no poison mix’d, no sharp-ground knife,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: No sudden mean of death, though ne’er so mean,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: But “banished” to kill me?—“banished”?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: O friar, the damned use that word in hell;

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Howlings attend it: how hast thou the heart,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Being a divine, a ghostly confessor,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: A sin-absolver, and my friend profess’d,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: To mangle me with that word “banished”?

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Thou fond mad man, hear me but speak a word.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: O, thou wilt speak again of banishment.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: I’ll give thee armour to keep off that word;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Adversity’s sweet milk, philosophy,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: To comfort thee, though thou art banished.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Yet “banished”? Hang up philosophy!

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Unless philosophy can make a Juliet,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Displant a town, reverse a prince’s doom,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: It helps not, it prevails not: talk no more.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: O, then I see that madmen have no ears.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: How should they, when that wise men have no eyes?

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Let me dispute with thee of thy estate.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Thou canst not speak of that thou dost not feel:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Wert thou as young as I, Juliet thy love,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: An hour but married, Tybalt murdered,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Doting like me and like me banished,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Then mightst thou speak, then mightst thou tear thy hair,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: And fall upon the ground, as I do now,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Taking the measure of an unmade grave.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Arise; one knocks; good Romeo, hide thyself.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Not I; unless the breath of heart-sick groans,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Mist-like, infold me from the search of eyes.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Hark, how they knock! Who’s there? Romeo, arise;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Thou wilt be taken. Stay awhile! Stand up;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Run to my study. By and by! God’s will,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: What simpleness is this! I come, I come!

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Who knocks so hard? whence come you? what’s your will?

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Let me come in, and you shall know my errand;

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: I come from Lady Juliet.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Welcome, then.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: O holy friar, O, tell me, holy friar,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Where is my lady’s lord, where’s Romeo?

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: There on the ground, with his own tears made drunk.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: O, he is even in my mistress’ case,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Just in her case! O woeful sympathy!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Piteous predicament! Even so lies she,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Blubbering and weeping, weeping and blubbering.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Stand up, stand up; stand, an you be a man:

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: For Juliet’s sake, for her sake, rise and stand;

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Why should you fall into so deep an O?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Nurse!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Ah sir! ah sir! Well, death’s the end of all.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Spakest thou of Juliet? how is it with her?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Doth she not think me an old murderer,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Now I have stain’d the childhood of our joy

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: With blood removed but little from her own?

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Where is she? and how doth she? and what says

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: My conceal’d lady to our cancell’d love?

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: O, she says nothing, sir, but weeps and weeps;

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: And now falls on her bed; and then starts up,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: And Tybalt calls; and then on Romeo cries,

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: And then down falls again.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: As if that name,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Shot from the deadly level of a gun,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Did murder her; as that name’s cursed hand

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Murder’d her kinsman. O, tell me, friar, tell me,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: In what vile part of this anatomy

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Doth my name lodge? tell me, that I may sack

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: The hateful mansion.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Hold thy desperate hand:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Art thou a man? thy form cries out thou art:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Thy tears are womanish; thy wild acts denote

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: The unreasonable fury of a beast:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Unseemly woman in a seeming man!

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Or ill-beseeming beast in seeming both!

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Thou hast amazed me: by my holy order,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: I thought thy disposition better temper’d.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Hast thou slain Tybalt? wilt thou slay thyself?

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And slay thy lady too that lives in thee,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: By doing damned hate upon thyself?

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Why rail’st thou on thy birth, the heaven, and earth?

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Since birth, and heaven, and earth, all three do meet

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: In thee at once; which thou at once wouldst lose.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Fie, fie, thou shamest thy shape, thy love, thy wit;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Which, like a usurer, abound’st in all,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And usest none in that true use indeed

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Which should bedeck thy shape, thy love, thy wit:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Thy noble shape is but a form of wax,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Digressing from the valour of a man;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Thy dear love sworn but hollow perjury,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Killing that love which thou hast vow’d to cherish;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Thy wit, that ornament to shape and love,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Mis-shapen in the conduct of them both,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Like powder in a skilless soldier’s flask,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Is set a-fire by thine own ignorance,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And thou dismember’d with thine own defence.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: What, rouse thee, man! thy Juliet is alive,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: For whose dear sake thou wast but lately dead;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: There art thou happy: Tybalt would kill thee,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: But thou slew’st Tybalt; there are thou happy too:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: The law that threaten’d death becomes thy friend

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And turns it to exile; there art thou happy:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: A pack of blessings lights up upon thy back;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Happiness courts thee in her best array;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: But, like a misbehaved and sullen wench,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Thou pout’st upon thy fortune and thy love:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Take heed, take heed, for such die miserable.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Go, get thee to thy love, as was decreed,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Ascend her chamber, hence and comfort her:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: But look thou stay not till the watch be set,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: For then thou canst not pass to Mantua;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Where thou shalt live, till we can find a time

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: To blaze your marriage, reconcile your friends,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Beg pardon of the prince, and call thee back

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: With twenty hundred thousand times more joy

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Than thou went’st forth in lamentation.

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Go before, nurse: commend me to thy lady;

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And bid her hasten all the house to bed,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Which heavy sorrow makes them apt unto:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Romeo is coming.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: O Lord, I could have stay’d here all the night

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: To hear good counsel: O, what learning is!

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: My lord, I’ll tell my lady you will come.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Do so, and bid my sweet prepare to chide.

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Here, sir, a ring she bid me give you, sir:

lines.speaker: NURSE
lines.text: Hie you, make haste, for it grows very late.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: How well my comfort is revived by this!

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Go hence; good night; and here stands all your state:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Either be gone before the watch be set,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Or by the break of day disguised from hence:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Sojourn in Mantua; I’ll find out your man,

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: And he shall signify from time to time

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Every good hap to you that chances here:

lines.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
lines.text: Give me thy hand; ’tis late: farewell; good night.

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: But that a joy past joy calls out on me,

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: It were a grief, so brief to part with thee:

lines.speaker: ROMEO
lines.text: Farewell.

glosses.definition: trembling man

glosses.phrase: affliction is enamour’d of thy parts
glosses.definition: misfortune is in love with your qualities — irony
misfortune
the Prince's softer sentence slipped from him
Romeo denies this term to his sentence
Juliet is his heaven; banishment is hell
worth; value
flies that feed on dead flesh
Romeo's envy of the freedom flies enjoy — to touch Juliet
philosophy as the nurse of those in trouble
enough of abstract consolation — Romeo's rejection of Stoic counsel
foolish; doting
the Friar rebukes Romeo for unmanly weeping
passionate, unreasonable behavior
the Nurse interrupts Romeo's collapse
the Nurse calls Romeo by his new identity — Tybalt's killer

empty oath-breaking — the Friar charges Romeo
misbehaving
money-lender — overflowing in true wealth yet withholding

ordained

here is the whole picture of your condition

before the city guard takes its posts
fortune; chance

annotations.title: 'There is no world without Verona walls' — banishment as death
annotations.body: Romeo's speech on banishment is the play's most extreme statement of the lover's absolutism. The Friar has announced the Prince's mercy: exile instead of execution. Romeo rejects the framing: 'There is no world without Verona walls, / But purgatory, torture, hell itself.' To be banished from Juliet is to be banished from existence. The speech stacks increasingly extreme formulations — banishment is worse than death, because death at least sets you free from Juliet's absence. This is hyperbole, but it is also an accurate report of how love-totalitarianism felt to a sixteen-year-old in Shakespeare's world (and in ours). The Friar's attempt to counsel 'philosophy' — classical Stoic acceptance — crashes into Romeo's 'Hang up philosophy.' The play is not endorsing either position; it is staging the collision.
sources: Douglas Peterson, Time, Tide, and Tempest (1973)

trials.prompt: Why does Romeo say 'There is no world without Verona walls'?
options: Because banishment means separation from Juliet — without her, there is no 'world' worth the name
Because Verona has the best food in Italy

trials.prompt: What position does the Friar defend against Romeo's despair?
options: He urges him to escape to France
He appeals to 'philosophy' — classical Stoic acceptance — which Romeo rejects ('Hang up philosophy')
He argues Romeo should confess and surrender
He urges Romeo to forget Juliet
